transcript: Yeah, so there was this coworker who kept missing deadlines and it was slowing down our whole team. I didn't manage them or anything, so I just went and talked to them about it. I explained how it was affecting everyone and they started being more on time after that. It worked out fine.
gaps: No STAR structure — missing Situation, Task, Action, Result
Zero specific detail: no names, numbers, timelines, or outcomes
No explanation of how the influence actually worked
one_specific_improvement: Open your next answer with a one-sentence Situation that names your role and the specific project — for example: I was coordinating a product launch and a peer engineer was missing deadlines.

transcript: At my previous job, I was on a cross-functional project team and one of my peers — a developer — kept delivering their pieces late. This created a bottleneck for the rest of us. Since I had no authority over them, I requested a one-on-one conversation. I came prepared with data showing how their delays were pushing back our shared milestones. I made sure to frame it around our common goal rather than pointing blame. After that conversation, they started flagging blockers earlier and we got back on track. The project delivered on time and the working relationship stayed positive.
strengths: Clear narrative arc with an implicit before/after
Good instinct to frame around shared goals rather than blame
gaps: No explicit STAR structure — Situation and Task are merged and thin
Specificity is still low: data and milestones are unnamed
Action section is brief — the conversation itself is not described
one_specific_improvement: Add one concrete detail to your Action: describe a single thing you said or showed in the conversation — for example, the visual or specific question you used to open the dialogue.

transcript: Situation: While working as a project coordinator on a product launch, I was part of a cross-functional team that included developers, designers, and a marketing lead. One of the senior developers was consistently delivering their modules two to three days late. Because I had no direct authority over them, I had to find another way to address it. Task: My goal was to get the project back on schedule without damaging the relationship or escalating to management, which I felt would create tension and slow things down further. Action: I reached out to schedule a casual one-on-one — not framed as a confrontation, but as a check-in. I came with a simple visual showing how the delays were cascading across the rest of the timeline. I asked open-ended questions to understand their challenges first, which uncovered that they were being pulled into last-minute support tickets by another team. Together, we worked out an agreement: they would block off mornings for project work and flag conflicts earlier. I also helped buffer some of their tasks by redistributing two non-critical items to other team members. Result: Over the following three weeks, their delivery cadence improved significantly. We shipped the product launch on time, and the developer later thanked me for approaching it the way I did. It built a stronger working relationship going forward.
strengths: Explicit STAR structure now present with clear labels
Good tactical detail in Action: visual, open-ended questions, task redistribution
gaps: Specificity still low — no numbers on timeline impact or buffer consumed
Confidence dips in Task: I felt would create tension is hedged reasoning
Result lacks a quantified outcome — significantly is vague
one_specific_improvement: Quantify the result: replace improved significantly with a concrete change — for example, they hit every milestone over the following three weeks or we recovered two weeks of buffer.

transcript: Situation: About two years ago, I was coordinating a high-visibility product launch at my company. The team spanned engineering, design, and marketing — none of whom reported to me. About six weeks before launch, I noticed that one of the senior developers was consistently missing internal deadlines by two to three days. Each slip was small on its own, but when compounded, it was threatening our launch date. Task: My job was to keep the project on track, but I had no formal authority to direct this person's work. Escalating to their manager felt like a last resort — it could create defensiveness and harm the collaborative dynamic we needed. So I needed to influence them through relationship and reasoning alone. Action: First, I did some homework. I mapped out exactly how each of their delays rippled through the rest of the project — the specific tasks that could not start, the reviewers who were blocked, the buffer days we were burning. Then I asked to grab coffee — low stakes, no agenda on the calendar invite. I started by acknowledging that they were clearly juggling a lot. That opened the door for them to share they were being pulled into urgent support requests by a different team mid-sprint. I showed them the cascade diagram and asked: What would make it easier to protect your time for this project? They came up with most of the solution themselves. I also worked with another team member to absorb two lower-priority deliverables. Result: Their delivery consistency improved dramatically. We launched on time. At the retrospective, the developer mentioned that the way I approached the conversation made them feel respected rather than called out.
strengths: Strong Action: cascade diagram, listening-first, solution co-created with the other person
Compelling Result with qualitative impact from retrospective feedback
gaps: STAR structure present but Situation is slightly long relative to the rest
Result still lacks a hard metric — on time is correct but the recovery is not described
Confidence language is good but felt like a last resort mildly hedges the Task
one_specific_improvement: Tighten Situation to two sentences, then use the saved time to add one hard number to Result — such as the number of sprints where they hit every deadline after the conversation.

transcript: Situation: About two years ago, I was the project coordinator for a major product launch with VP-level visibility. The team was cross-functional: two engineers, a UX designer, a QA lead, and a marketing manager — none of whom reported to me. Around the six-week mark, one of the senior engineers was delivering pieces two to three days late consistently. Each slip was eating into our testing window and cascading into the design review and marketing build-out. Task: I needed to change this person's behavior and protect the timeline — without authority to direct them, and without damaging the relationship. Action: I built a timeline visual showing the original plan versus current state, with annotations on which tasks were blocked. I asked them for a casual coffee — invite said only Wanted to sync on the project. I opened by asking how things were going. They told me they were being pulled into P1 support incidents by another team with no advance notice. That reframed everything. I shared the visual as shared context, then asked: What would help you protect time for this project? They drove the solution: blocking mornings, flagging slips by end of day. I absorbed two of their lower-priority deliverables by redistributing them, and set up a 15-minute weekly check-in. Result: Delivery improved within the first week. When one external incident arose, they flagged it two days early and we adjusted without crisis. We launched on schedule. At the retrospective, the engineer said unprompted that I came in curious rather than critical, and that it made them want to come through for the team. Influencing without authority is about earning it — by understanding someone's reality before trying to change their behavior.
strengths: Full STAR with precise detail throughout — VP visibility, named roles, specific timeline visual
Listening-first approach clearly demonstrated; solution co-created not imposed
Closing insight elevates the answer to a leadership principle
gaps: Conciseness could tighten: closing reflection slightly extends past the ideal 4-minute mark
one_specific_improvement: Cut the closing reflection to one sentence delivered after the Result — the insight lands harder when sharp. End at made them want to come through for the team and drop everything after.
